quick_facts.label: Core qualification
quick_facts.value: A recognised BDS qualification is the usual entry credential for government Dental Surgeon posts; the National Dental Commission’s published BDS regulations include four professional examinations followed by a one-year paid rotating internship.

quick_facts.label: Professional registration
quick_facts.value: Recruitment rules and advertisements commonly require registration with a State Dental Council or the national dental regulator; candidates should confirm the exact registration wording in the applicable notification.

quick_facts.label: Appointment routes
quick_facts.value: Posts may be filled through regular state health-service/commission recruitment or through contractual National Health Mission and National Oral Health Programme engagements.

quick_facts.label: Public-health role
quick_facts.value: Under the National Oral Health Programme, dental surgeons are among the human resources supported for dental care units and integrated oral-health care at district-level and lower public facilities.

quick_facts.label: Pay context
quick_facts.value: Pay is employer- and appointment-type-specific. For example, a 2022 Mizoram PSC advertisement listed Level 10 at ₹56,100–₹1,24,500 for Grade-III Dental Surgeon posts, while a 2025 NHM Wayanad contractual notice listed ₹41,000 per month. These are dated examples, not a national salary standard.

sections.heading: Responsibilities in public dental care
paragraphs: A Government Dental Surgeon provides oral-health care within the scope of the appointing service and facility. The National Oral Health Programme (NOHP) is designed to deliver integrated and comprehensive oral-health care through existing public facilities, with objectives that include reducing oral disease, integrating promotion and prevention with general health care, and supporting dental units at district level or below.

A post may combine clinical care with public-health activity. NOHP support includes dental-care-unit manpower, equipment, consumables, training and information/education/communication activity; a government employment notice from VSSC describes the core duty as providing dental treatment to covered beneficiaries. The exact clinical workload, outreach obligations, reporting duties and referral arrangements are determined by the employing department and its notification.
bullets: Provide examination, diagnosis, treatment and referral within the facility’s authorised dental services.
Contribute to preventive oral-health promotion and public-health delivery where assigned under NOHP/NHM.
Work with dental assistants, hygienists, technicians and the wider public-health team where those roles are available.

sections.heading: How entry commonly works
paragraphs: The normal foundation is a recognised BDS degree and completion of the compulsory rotating internship. The published BDS regulations set out four professional examinations and then require every successful Final BDS candidate to undergo a one-year paid rotating internship.

Applicants must meet the particular employer’s eligibility conditions. For example, the Delhi Health Service Dental Surgeon recruitment-rules document specifies a recognised BDS qualification, registration with a State Dental Council or tribunal in India, and completion of compulsory rotating internship before appointment; the Mizoram PSC advertisement similarly required BDS and permanent registration. Age limits, domicile or language requirements, reservation, experience, computer requirements and document-verification rules are jurisdiction-specific.

Regular posts may use a public-service commission or other authorised recruitment process. Separately, NOHP guidance expressly allows contractual appointment of dental surgeons where manpower is required, and district NHM notices continue to advertise Dental Surgeon posts under NOHP and NUHM. Candidates should therefore monitor the relevant State/UT PSC, health department, NHM and district recruitment portals rather than assume a single national examination route.
bullets: Complete BDS and compulsory rotating internship.
Obtain and maintain the registration required by the recruiting authority.
Apply against the specific regular or contractual notification and complete its selection process, which may include a written examination, interview, merit process or other prescribed stages.
Treat each notice as controlling: eligibility and appointment conditions differ between states, Union Territories and contractual programmes.

sections.heading: Knowledge and skills developed for the role
paragraphs: BDS training spans the clinical and scientific base needed for general dental practice: oral medicine and radiology, oral surgery, conservative dentistry and endodontics, periodontics, prosthodontics, orthodontics, paediatric dentistry, community dentistry, general medicine and general surgery. The curriculum also includes clinical/practical work and community-dentistry field exposure in rural, semi-urban or urban underserved settings.

For a government-service setting, this translates into the ability to deliver safe general dental care, identify cases needing referral, keep clinical records, communicate preventive advice, and work in a public-health team. The balance between chairside practice, outreach and administration depends on the posting and programme.
bullets: Clinical assessment, diagnosis and treatment planning across general dentistry.
Safe procedural practice, infection-control discipline and appropriate referral judgement.
Patient communication and preventive oral-health counselling.
Community-orientation and collaboration with multidisciplinary public-health personnel.

sections.heading: Where Government Dental Surgeons work
paragraphs: Government Dental Surgeons can work in public dental care units located at district hospitals and facilities below district level. The Directorate General of Health Services identifies NOHP-supported dental care units across district hospitals, sub-district hospitals, community health centres, primary health centres, Ayushman Arogya Mandirs and mobile dental units.

Employment may be within a regular state or Union Territory health-service cadre, or on an NHM/NOHP/NUHM contractual engagement. A 2026 District Bastar NHM recruitment notice, for example, listed both Dental Surgeon–NOHP and Dental Surgeon–NUHM vacancies, illustrating that the programme and location materially shape the post.
bullets: District and sub-district public hospitals.
Community health centres, primary health centres and other lower-level public facilities with dental units.
Urban public-health settings under NUHM.
Mobile or outreach-oriented dental services where established by the state or Union Territory.

sections.heading: Progression and specialisation
paragraphs: Career progression is governed by the relevant service rules rather than by a uniform India-wide pathway. The Mizoram Health Service framework distinguishes entry-level Dental Surgeon Sub-Cadre Grade III from Dental Specialist Sub-Cadre Grade II; the latter requires BDS plus an MDS/equivalent relevant specialty qualification, or a postgraduate diploma/equivalent with specified experience. This is an example of how postgraduate dental qualifications can support movement into specialist government roles.

Some departmental cadre structures also provide graded advancement. The published Delhi Health Service Dental Surgeon document presents Dental Surgeon as an entry grade followed by Junior Staff Surgeon (Dental), Staff Surgeon (Dental), Staff Surgeon (Dental) (NFSG), and Consultant in Dental Surgery; it is explicitly labelled a draft document, so it should be read only as an example of a jurisdiction-specific hierarchy, not as a universally applicable rule.
bullets: Build regular service experience under the applicable cadre rules.
Pursue MDS or another recognised postgraduate pathway when targeting specialist posts.
Check the particular service’s promotion rules, qualifying service, performance requirements and vacancy/financial-upgradation provisions.

sections.heading: Compensation context
paragraphs: There is no single national pay figure for a Government Dental Surgeon. Regular cadre roles may be placed in a state or Union Territory pay matrix, while NHM and NOHP appointments can be fixed-term contractual engagements with a consolidated monthly remuneration. The employer’s recruitment notice, service rules, appointment category and applicable allowance orders control the actual package.

As dated reference points only: Mizoram PSC’s 2022 Grade-III Dental Surgeon advertisement stated Pay Level 10 of ₹56,100–₹1,24,500; a 2025 NHM Wayanad notice stated ₹41,000 for a Dental Surgeon vacancy; and the Delhi Health Service draft listed Level 10 at ₹56,100–₹1,77,500 for its Dental Surgeon entry grade. These examples are not directly comparable and should not be used to predict a current offer elsewhere.
bullets: Verify whether the post is regular, temporary, outsourced or contractual.
Read the notification for pay level or consolidated remuneration, tenure, probation, allowances and service benefits.
Use the advertisement’s date and the appointing authority’s current orders when comparing compensation.
